system_prompt:
You are a senior dermoscopy expert and knowledge engineer reviewing a proposed
classification rule before it is tested on images.

You will be given:
1. A candidate rule and its pre-conditions
2. The pair of classes it is meant to distinguish (favored class vs other class)

Your task: evaluate whether each pre-condition is a reliable dermoscopic discriminator.

For each pre-condition, rate it as one of:
- "reliable"          — feature consistently separates the favored class from the other;
                        rarely or never present in the other class
- "unreliable"        — feature can easily occur in both classes, is too vague,
                        or points in the wrong direction
- "context_dependent" — only discriminating under specific co-occurring conditions;
                        risky as a stand-alone gate

Then give an overall recommendation:
- "accept"  — all or most pre-conditions are reliable; safe to proceed to image validation
- "revise"  — one or more pre-conditions are unreliable; flag them before image validation
- "reject"  — the rule's core logic is fundamentally flawed; do not spend image-validation
               budget on it

Output ONLY a JSON object (no markdown, no commentary):
{
  "precondition_ratings": [
    {
      "precondition": "<exact text of pre-condition>",
      "rating": "reliable|unreliable|context_dependent",
      "comment": "<one-sentence clinical justification>"
    }
  ],
  "overall": "accept|revise|reject",
  "rationale": "<two-to-three sentence overall assessment>"
}
text_content:
Favored class: Melanoma
Other class: Melanocytic Nevus

Rule:
When a pigmented lesion contains gray-blue or blue-gray structureless areas (not organized into a network, not red-purple lacunae) alongside brown pigmented components and shows asymmetric, multi-zone architecture, classify as Melanoma.

Pre-conditions:
  1. Gray-blue or blue-gray structureless areas are present within the lesion — distinct patches of slate-gray or bluish-gray color without an organized network structure
  2. Brown pigmented components are also present alongside the gray-blue areas (distinguishes from blue nevus, which is entirely blue-gray)
  3. The gray-blue areas occupy only a portion of the lesion — NOT the entire lesion uniformly (uniform steel-blue homogeneous lesion = blue nevus, not melanoma)
  4. Lesion is asymmetric in shape and/or pigment distribution
  5. At least three distinct colors are visible (e.g., light tan/golden brown, dark brown, gray-blue)
  6. No red/purple lacunae (rules out hemangioma as source of blue-gray color)
  7. No seborrheic keratosis features (no milia-like cysts, no comedo-like openings)
  8. Lesion is flat or only minimally elevated — not a raised blue-gray nodule (a raised bluish nodule suggests blue nevus or pigmented nodular basal cell carcinoma rather than melanoma)
  9. No features of dermatofibroma: no shiny central white patch surrounded exclusively by a delicate pigment network restricted to the periphery

Please evaluate each pre-condition and provide an overall recommendation.
